sourceTitle: Best practices for internal communications on Notion

questions.question: Why does the guide recommend creating a "what's new" database?

options.text: To store all types of updates—newsletters, memos, and all‑hands notes—in one place
options.explanation: A central "what's new" database consolidates important updates, making them easy to find and reducing the risk of information getting lost.

options.text: To replace the company wiki entirely
options.explanation: The database complements the wiki; it doesn't replace the wiki, which still holds foundational pages like mission and values.

options.text: To automatically publish updates to social media
options.explanation: The guide does not mention automatic social media publishing; the database is for internal visibility.

options.text: To store only meeting notes for each team
options.explanation: Meeting notes are a separate use case; the "what's new" database is for broader company‑wide announcements.

questions.question: Which visual element does the guide suggest using first to capture a reader’s attention?

options.text: A divider
options.explanation: Dividers create visual breaks but are not the first element to capture attention.

options.text: An image, video, or embed
options.explanation: The guide states that visuals are the first thing a reader notices and should be used to highlight key points.

options.text: A callout block
options.explanation: Callouts are useful for emphasis but come after an initial visual hook.

options.text: A colored heading
options.explanation: Color adds emphasis but is not highlighted as the primary attention‑grabber.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of using a synced block for the company mission statement?

options.text: It lets you edit the mission in one place and have the change appear everywhere the block is used
options.explanation: Synced blocks propagate updates automatically, eliminating the need to edit each instance manually.

options.text: It automatically translates the mission statement into multiple languages
options.explanation: Synced blocks do not provide translation capabilities.

options.text: It locks the mission statement so no one can edit it
options.explanation: Synced blocks are editable; locking is a separate permission setting.

options.text: It creates a backup copy of the mission statement in a separate database
options.explanation: Synced blocks are about live duplication, not backup copies.

questions.question: How does the guide suggest you should structure a company‑wide announcement template?

options.text: Include sections for "Context" and "Next steps" to cover edge cases
options.explanation: The guide specifically mentions these sections as a way to ensure consistency and completeness.

options.text: Leave the template completely blank so writers can start from scratch each time
options.explanation: A blank template defeats the purpose of consistency and guidance.

options.text: Add a large image at the top and no text sections
options.explanation: While visuals are encouraged, an announcement still needs structured textual information.

options.text: Only include a checklist of tasks without any explanatory text
options.explanation: Checklists are useful but announcements need context and next steps, not just tasks.

questions.question: Which Notion feature allows you to automatically push a page update to a Slack channel?

options.text: Connect a Slack Channel
options.explanation: The guide mentions the "Connect a Slack Channel" feature for sending Notion updates directly to Slack.

options.text: Database Template Button
options.explanation: Template buttons create new content inside Notion, not external notifications.

options.text: Public Share Link
options.explanation: Public links make a page viewable on the web, but they don't push to Slack.

options.text: Synced Block
options.explanation: Synced blocks duplicate content within Notion, not to external apps.

questions.question: Why does the guide recommend using toggles in communication pages?

options.text: To hide optional details and let readers expand only what they need
options.explanation: Toggles let you present high‑level points while allowing interested readers to drill down into details.

options.text: To create columns automatically
options.explanation: Columns are created by dragging blocks, not by toggles.

options.text: To embed external videos without using the /embed command
options.explanation: Videos are embedded via the /embed block, not toggles.

options.text: To change the background color of a section
options.explanation: Background colors are applied via the color menu, not toggles.

questions.question: What problem does establishing a company‑wide operating system (OS) for foundational pages solve?

options.text: It centralizes mission, values, and core philosophies so new and existing teammates can quickly find them
options.explanation: A company‑wide OS provides a single source of truth for foundational information, reducing confusion.

options.text: It eliminates the need for any team‑specific wikis
options.explanation: Team wikis still exist; the OS complements them by housing cross‑team fundamentals.

options.text: It automatically generates weekly newsletters
options.explanation: Newsletter generation is a separate process; the OS is about static foundational content.

options.text: It replaces all databases with plain pages
options.explanation: Databases remain valuable for structured data; the OS is a page hierarchy, not a database replacement.

questions.question: When might you choose a Template Button over a Database Template?

options.text: When you need to add a small, repeatable item (like a weekly to‑do list) without creating a full new page each time
options.explanation: Template buttons generate inline content, ideal for short, recurring items, whereas database templates create new records/pages.

options.text: When you want to create a new team wiki from scratch
options.explanation: A full wiki is better built with a page template or the Template Gallery, not a button.

options.text: When you need to sync content across multiple pages automatically
options.explanation: Synced blocks handle cross‑page synchronization, not template buttons.

options.text: When you want to embed a Typeform survey
options.explanation: Embedding a Typeform uses the /Typeform block, unrelated to template buttons.

questions.question: According to the guide, what is a key benefit of making a page public?

options.text: External stakeholders like investors can stay up‑to‑date without needing a Notion account
options.explanation: Public pages can be accessed via a web link, allowing anyone (including investors) to view updates without a Notion login.

options.text: The page becomes searchable on Google and improves SEO for the company
options.explanation: Public pages are not automatically indexed for search engine optimization.

options.text: All internal team members automatically receive email notifications
options.explanation: Public sharing only affects external access; internal notifications depend on sharing settings and mentions.

options.text: It locks the page so no one can edit it
options.explanation: Public sharing controls view access, not edit permissions; you can still allow editing if you choose.

questions.question: What purpose does a callout serve in a Notion communication page?

options.text: To highlight important information and give the section a clear purpose
options.explanation: Callouts draw attention to key points and can be used to label what a section is about.

options.text: To create a new database automatically
options.explanation: Callouts are visual blocks, not database creation tools.

options.text: To embed a video without using the /embed command
options.explanation: Videos are embedded via the /embed block; callouts are for emphasis, not media embedding.

options.text: To add a horizontal line between sections
options.explanation: Dividers create horizontal lines; callouts provide highlighted text.
